data.title: ZAWYA: Cambridge Health Group expands Jeddah Hospital to 200 beds
data.description:
Jeddah | Cambridge Health Group ("CHG"), the GCC's leading post-acute care provider and a subsidiary of Amanat Holdings PJSC ("Amanat"), today announced the inauguration of its fully refurbished and expanded Cambridge Hospital Jeddah, now a 200-bed specialist facility operating at full capacity.

This expansion reinforces Cambridge Health Group’s position as the largest post-acute care provider in the GCC with over 700 beds across its network and reflects its continued commitment to expanding high-quality healthcare services across the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, United Arab Emirates and the wider region.

The Jeddah hospital has been comprehensively upgraded and expanded from 130 to 200 beds, introducing enhanced patient rooms, advanced clinical infrastructure and a significantly expanded rehabilitation offering, including dedicated gym facilities delivering a patient centric environment. The development builds on Cambridge’s recent expansion trajectory in the Kingdom, including the launch of a 150-bed long-term care facility in Khobar in 2024 and a 70-bed rehabilitation hospital in Dhahran in 2019.

The announcement also follows the Group’s recently disclosed plans to further expand the Jeddah facility by an additional 70 beds, alongside the introduction of outpatient and surgical capabilities. This will position Cambridge Hospital Jeddah as the leading post-acute and rehabilitative care provider in the Western Region.

The inauguration was attended by Dr. Ali Saeed bin Harmal Aldhaheri, Chairman of Cambridge Health Group, alongside distinguished representatives of the shareholders. During the visit, the delegation toured the refurbished hospital and spent time with patients and their families, who shared warm feedback on the new patient environment and the advanced rehabilitation services.

Dr. Ali Saeed bin Harmal Aldhaheri, Chairman of Cambridge Health Group, said:

"Saudi Arabia is a core growth market for Cambridge Health Group. This expansion reflects our long-term commitment to the Kingdom and our confidence in the continued development of its healthcare sector. With over 700 beds across the GCC and a clear pipeline for further expansion, we are proud to be building the region’s leading post-acute care provider and delivering world-class clinical outcomes at scale."

Dr. Mohammed Halawani, KSA Chief Executive Officer of Cambridge Health Group, said:

"The Jeddah hospital is operating at full capacity, reflecting both the quality of care we provide and the significant demand for specialised post-acute and rehabilitation services in the Kingdom. This expansion, alongside our ongoing development pipeline, positions us to further enhance patient access and align with the Kingdom's healthcare transformation agenda under Vision 2030."

The expansion comes on the back of a record year for Cambridge Health Group, with strong financial performance across its network and sustained high utilisation levels, underlining the growing demand for specialised post-acute care services across the GCC.

About Cambridge Health Group

Cambridge Health Group is the GCC’s leading provider of post-acute care, rehabilitation, long-term care, and home healthcare services, with more than 700 beds and a broad network of outpatient and home healthcare services. The Group operates six facilities across the United Arab Emirates and the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia and employs more than 1,200 healthcare professionals.

The Group has also received international and national accreditations from Joint Commission International (JCI), the Commission on Accreditation of Rehabilitation Facilities (CARF), the Saudi Central Board for Accreditation of Healthcare Institutions (CBAHI), and the College of American Pathologists (CAP), and is majority-owned by Amanat Holdings PJSC.

For Media Enquiries:

Communications Team | Cambridge Health Group

Communications@Cambridge-health.com

Send us your press releases to pressrelease.zawya@lseg.com

data.title: French pay-TV group Canal+ to list in South Africa on June 3
data.description:
Pay-TV group Canal+ said on Tuesday it would become the first French company to list in South Africa when it joins the Johannesburg stock exchange on June 3, fulfilling a commitment made when it bought the country's MultiChoice Group last year.

Canal+, which has a primary listing in London, said it made a "solid start" to 2026, with broadly flat revenue, and it reiterated its guidance for the year.

Shares in Canal+, which fell 17% last month when it revealed the challenge it faces in reviving MultiChoice, rose 7.5% in early deals.

Chief Executive Maxime Saada said the first steps in the MultiChoice turnaround had launched, including strengthening its commercial operation and recruiting new sales teams.

Total group revenue fell 0.4% in the first quarter on a restated basis to take into account the contribution from MultiChoice, the company said.

data.title: Hungarian Forint Could Rise if Central Bank Sounds Cautious About Rate Cuts — Market Talk
data.description:
0715 GMT - The Hungarian forint could rise if Hungary's central bank dampens expectations for interest-rate cuts in a decision at 1200 GMT, ING's Frantisek Taborsky says in a note. The central bank is expected to leave rates at 6.25% so the focus is on the forward guidance. Uncertainty over the Iran war means Hungary is unlikely to rush to cut rates, which could help the forint, he says. However, gains could prove limited as the central bank doesn't have much market influence in the current environment, he says. More attention may be paid to incoming Prime Minister Peter Magyar's meeting in Brussels on Wednesday and on the potential unlocking of EU funds, he says. The euro rises 0.9% to 365.50 forints. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0653 GMT - Bitcoin edges lower as tensions between the U.S. and Iran show no signs of abating, leaving investors cautious. The Wall Street Journal reports that a U.S. official said President Trump is skeptical of Iran's latest proposal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Trump sounded notes about Iran not dealing in good faith or being willing to meet his key demand of ending nuclear enrichment and vowing to never make a nuclear weapon. Markets "have been latching on to any signs of peace talks, and the absence of that is raising fears they're not going to happen," Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. Bitcoin falls 0.2% to $76,790, LSEG data show. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0646 GMT - The BOJ's sharp upward revision to the inflation forecast and clear downgrade to the growth projection for the fiscal year ending March 2027 imply that the timing of further rate hikes remains heavily dependent on the situation in the Middle East, SMBC Nikko Securities economists say. In its quarterly report, the BOJ said "it will consider the timing and pace of adjustment, while closely monitoring the impact of the future course of the situation in the Middle East on Japan's economic activity and prices." The economists project that the BOJ will raise rates in June but caution that the central bank could delay if energy shortages worsen. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0644 GMT - The damage to government bond markets from the war in the Middle East could prove longer-lasting than that to equities, says Capital Economics in a note to clients. Most of the rise in yields this year reflects changes in expectations for monetary policy. The key question, therefore, is whether the war has had a lasting effect on the path for monetary policy, it adds. It appears as though the answer is yes, Capital Economics says. It's now unlikely the Fed or the Bank of England will cut this year, versus the expectation before the war. Current market pricing broadly supports the scenario. That suggests that a broad-based bond rally is unlikely even if the mood improves, it says. (james.glynn@wsj.com; X @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0643 GMT - Eurozone government bond yields rise in opening trade, tracking the direction of U.S. Treasurys. Tuesday's eurozone bond supply schedule is light, with only the Netherlands conducting an auction. The economic data calendar is moderate too before it picks up on Wednesday, though the European Central Bank's bank lending survey and consumer expectations survey are due for release. The 10-year German Bund yield rises 1.3 basis points to 3.049%, and yield increases on other countries' 10-year bonds is of approximately the same magnitude, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0639 GMT - The dollar rises after a U.S. official said Monday that President Trump is skeptical about Iran's latest proposal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and end the war. Trump sounded notes about Iran not dealing in good faith or being willing to meet his key demand of ending nuclear enrichment and vowing to never make a nuclear weapon, the WSJ reports. The latest news lifts oil prices, supporting the dollar as the U.S. is a net oil exporter. The DXY dollar index rises 0.1% to 98.613. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0619 GMT - The Bank of Japan's policy decision was a "hawkish hold" that leaves the door open for a June rate hike, says Daiwa Securities economist Kento Minami. The BOJ upgraded its price projections in its quarterly report and said underlying inflation is expected to reach its target between the second half of fiscal 2026 and fiscal 2027. "The latest price outlook signals a heightened sense of vigilance regarding upside inflation risks," Minami says. "The hawkish tone of the outlook report underscores the BOJ's firm commitment to proceed with further interest rate hikes," he adds. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0609 GMT - The BOJ's hawkish rate hold is likely a "warning shot" to markets, Saxo Markets' Charu Chanana says in a commentary. "The Middle East shock is no longer just a geopolitical risk for markets; it is becoming a central bank risk," the chief investment strategist says. "If inflation pressure arrives before growth weakness becomes undeniable, policymakers may tighten or stay restrictive for longer than markets expect." This sustains upward pressure on short-dated bond yields, complicates the case for equities and makes the next round of global central bank meetings more important than usual, Chanana adds. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0604 GMT - The Bank of Japan appears increasingly wary of the risk of falling behind the curve on inflation, says Mitsubishi UFJ Morgan Stanley Securities strategist Naomi Muguruma. In its report issued Tuesday, the BOJ said it needs to avoid a spike in inflation that could cool the economy, given that underlying consumer inflation is approaching the 2% target and companies are more actively passing on higher costs to wages and prices. "It shows that even though the economy is slowing down [due to the impact of the Middle East conflict], there will be no major change to the BOJ's path toward achieving the 2% target," Muguruma says. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0603 GMT - Discomfort from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is "slowly amplifying," as the 10-year U.S. breakeven inflation rate is edging towards 2.5%, ING's Padhraic Garvey says in a note. "Far from catastrophic, but if it were to break above, it would be a growing problem, for both Treasuries and the Federal Reserve," the regional head of research, Americas, says in a note. The entire inflation curve shifted higher through Monday, in tandem with an edge higher in the price of oil, Garvey says. The 10-year inflation breakeven hit its highest level--at 2.45%--since the war broke, he says. "It's, in fact, been a tad surprising that longer tenor inflation had not seen a more marked rise so far." (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0558 GMT - The Bank of Japan's 6-3 vote to keep the policy rate unchanged was interpreted by some market participants as a signal that the central bank will raise rates soon. While board members Hajime Takata and Naoki Tamura are considered supporters of tighter policy, Junko Nakagawa's opposition came as a surprise, as she is seen as more of a centrist. "Nakagawa's dissent may have been a strategic move to signal to the public that support for rate hikes is gaining traction across the board, rather than being confined to the usual two hawkish members," says Mizuho Securities economist Yusuke Matsuo. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0552 GMT - U.S. Treasury yields edge higher in a steepening manner--with short-end yields rising less than long-end yields--as oil prices climb in Asian trading. Treasury investors are focused on the Federal Reserve's coming meeting, where policy rates are expected to stay on hold. "All eyes will be on [Jerome] Powell's press conference, likely his last as Fed chair," says First Citizens Wealth's Phil Neuhart. "Markets are less focused on FOMC actions and more on the signal: is elevated inflation a transitory energy story or something more persistent?" the head of market and economic research says. The two-year Treasury yield is up 0.6 basis points at 3.809%, and the 10-year yield is 1.4 basis points higher at 4.349%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: European shares edge lower as US-Iran talks stall; corporate earnings on tap
data.description:
European shares edged lower on Tuesday as investors braced for a week packed with central bank meetings and corporate earnings, and also weighed a diplomatic impasse in U.S.-Iran negotiations.

Investor caution increased after U.S. officials indicated that U.S. President Donald Trump was dissatisfied with Iran's latest proposal on resolving the two-month-long war. The latest proposal sought to postpone a discussion on Iran's nuclear programme until the war concludes and shipping disputes are resolved.

The pan-European STOXX 600 was down 0.3% at 606.94 points, as of 0704 GMT.

The war has roiled global markets, driving oil prices higher and reigniting concerns over global inflation and growth, as the vital Strait of Hormuz remains closed.

Wall Street and other global markets have rebounded from a sharp selloff in March, while energy-dependent European stocks remain below pre-war levels.

With corporate earnings in full swing, shares of BP gained 2.3% after the energy giant posted first-quarter profit above expectations.

Shares of Novartis dropped 4.5% as the Swiss drugmaker reported quarterly core operating profit and sales below market estimates.

Norwegian Air Shuttle climbed roughly 4% after the budget airline reported a lower-than-expected operating loss, helped by a stronger crown, jet fuel hedges and the reduced price of EU Emissions Trading System (ETS) allowances.
